content:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗼𝗺 -- 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗵𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆.

Before 𝗞𝗲𝘀𝗮𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮 𝗕𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶 became a judgment, it was a constitutional storm.

The question was frighteningly simple:

Can Parliament amend the Constitution so completely that the Constitution itself loses its soul?

At the centre of this storm stood 𝗡𝗮𝗻𝗶 𝗣𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗵𝗶𝘃𝗮𝗹𝗮.

There was a moment when Palkhivala was searching for the legal foundation of his argument. The Constitution did not contain an express “eternity clause” like Germany. There was no direct Indian precedent strong enough to say that Parliament could amend -- but not destroy -- the Constitution.

Then, according to accounts of the case, 𝗔𝗻𝗶𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝘃𝗮𝗻 walked in and placed before him an article by German constitutional scholar 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗿 𝗗𝗶𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗿𝗮𝗱:

“𝗟𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗺𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿.”

That article carried a powerful idea:

A constitutional power created by the Constitution cannot be used to destroy the very foundation on which that power rests.

This idea had already appeared earlier in Indian constitutional litigation. In 𝗚𝗼𝗹𝗮𝗸𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗵, 𝗠.𝗞. 𝗡𝗮𝗺𝗯𝘆𝗮𝗿 had drawn from Conrad’s theory of implied limitations. The Court did not fully accept it then.

But ideas sometimes wait for their historic hour.

That hour came in 𝗞𝗲𝘀𝗮𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮.

The German background made the idea even deeper. After the collapse of the Weimar Constitution and the rise of the Nazi regime through legal and constitutional manipulation, Germany’s 1949 Basic Law inserted Article 79(3), the famous 𝗘𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘂𝘀𝗲.

It declared that certain fundamentals -- human dignity, democracy, federalism, rule of law -- could not be amended away.

India had no such written clause.

So Palkhivala asked the Court to discover it from the Constitution’s own spirit.

His argument was not that Parliament is powerless.

His argument was that power has limits.

Amendment is not annihilation.

Reform is not destruction.

Majority is not constitutional omnipotence.

A Constitution is not merely paper. It is the moral architecture of a republic.

And from that argument emerged the 𝗕𝗮𝘀𝗶𝗰 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗗𝗼𝗰𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗲 -- India’s invisible eternity clause.

It tells every government:

You may change the Constitution.

You may improve it.

You may adapt it to time.

But you cannot destroy its identity.

Perhaps that is why a line attributed to Justice 𝗛.𝗥. 𝗞𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗮 still echoes in Indian legal memory:

“It was not Nani who spoke. It was DIVINITY SPEAKING through him. Such arguments will not be heard in this Court for CENTURIES TO COME.”
-------- 𝗗𝗿 𝗡𝗲𝗲𝗹 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗶 𝗧𝗿𝗶𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗶
𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗵𝗼𝗿 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝗕𝗲𝘀𝘁𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗕𝗼𝗼𝗸  - amzn.in/d/01ewUFwD
